data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Power Metals, Verses AI, TFS Financial, Nextdoor +
data.description:
Undercovered Dozen Introduction

The 'Undercovered' Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this series provides ideas and inspires discussion.

Today, we're looking at articles published between March 28 - April 3.

Take a look at what these less-covered stock ideas might hold for you. And please join the conversation below: are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Kubota Is A Great Company Trading At A Fair Price

Jim Wade | Buy

Kubota Corporation (OTCPK:KUBTY) (OTCPK:KUBTF) is a Japanese agricultural [AG] equipment manufacturer with a long and varied history. Founded in 1890 as a producer of cast iron pipes, the company went on to build engines in the 1920s and eventually started producing ag equipment in the 1960s. Kubota today derives about 90% of revenue from their equipment business. The remainder comes from the Water & Environment segment, which produces pipes, pumps and filters for municipal water systems. I’ll be focusing primarily on the machinery segment, which will be the primary driver of Kubota's results.

Kubota is a well-known ag equipment manufacturer, but you’re not likely to see their orange tractors pulling a 32-row planter. The company’s focus lies in compact equipment – the type used for landscaping, hobby farming, small construction and specialty farming (think vineyards, orchards, nut farms, etc.) Kubota faces strong competition from a number of original equipment manufacturers [OEMs] in this space, including Deere (DE), CNH Industrial (CNH), AGCO (AGCO), and Doosan Bobcat. A review of their products and those of their competitors reveals that Kubota equipment stands out on quality and reliability. After spending a few hours going through online tractor reviews (Google will be serving me tractor ads for months), many people seem to pick Kubota equipment for these reasons: 1. Quality and reliability are favorably perceived; 2. Similar products are priced slightly below manufacturers like Deere; 3. Replacement parts are widely available; 4. Dealership proximity (1,200 dealerships nationwide) and service

Think of Kubota as the Toyota of compact equipment. They won’t be the cheapest option, but their equipment’s long-term value proposition earns them many repeat customers.

Northern Dynasty Minerals: Speculative Bet On Trump's Political Will

Daniel Schönberger | Hold

While this is not my first analysis of a mining company, the following article will probably be unlike any other analysis I have written in the last few years. For starters, I am writing about a business not generating any revenue at this time – and I usually cover businesses generating revenue on a consistent basis. I am also writing about a business in a very speculative stage and while we can see similar patterns in biotech companies with drugs only in Phase I, Phase II or Phase III, the following article will be somewhat unique.

Northern Dynasty Minerals Ltd. (NAK)(NDM:CA) is a speculative mining company not generating revenue, relying on financing through royalty agreements and potential future share issuances. The company's stock surged due to optimism around Trump's pro-mining policies, but past trends show potential for future disappointment. Rising global copper demand, especially from AI applications, could benefit Northern Dynasty, but significant capital expenditures and time are required before profitability.

When looking at the share price in the last few months, we see the stock price increasing since early October 2024 – from about 30 cents to $1.16 resulting in a stock price increase of almost 300%. One could argue that the stock started increasing when it became more and more obvious that Trump has a good chance to win the election in November.

The investment is highly speculative with a long-term horizon; I rate Northern Dynasty as a "Hold" due to high risks and uncertain returns.

Power Metals Is An Interesting Bet But A Bet

Tim Worstall | Buy

Of course, betting can be more or less informed, but there are simply certain areas of life that are bets. There are odds, probabilities, the more we know, the more we can nail those down. And yet, we still end up with that truth that never do all probabilities go the way the odds indicate. This is fine, obviously, the trick in investing is to get more right than wrong and thereby grow our total pot over time.

Junior miners, junior mining, is riskier than many other areas of life or markets. This is not something that we can ever escape, either. For the basic activity of a junior mining company is to uncover that more information to allow us to sharpen our appreciation of the odds. That's what they're actually doing.

Power Metals Corp. (PWM:CA)(PWRMF) is doing something markedly different. That makes them fun just for that and also, potentially at least, removes them from the more normal wild booms and busts that happen in resource extraction. They're exploring Case Lake at present. They own 100% of the claim, they've done a lot of the initial work. They're doing the work to prepare the mineral resource estimate.

This discovery here seems dominated by cesium. So, Power Metals are thinking of it as a cesium mine with lithium and tantalum byproduct. Seems sensible enough, but it's also true that the cesium market is pretty small. 3,000 tonnes a year globally, they seem to think. It's further true, as USGS points out, that the majority of usage is from recycling. What this means is that the current absence of a source isn't a huge problem. Also, that the opening of a new one will be to top up that circulating stock.

Verses AI: Early Volatility Accompanies Proposed Nasdaq Uplisting

Bull & Bear Trading | Hold

Verses AI, Inc. (OTCQB:VRSSD)(VRSSF) is a Canadian software company whose stock has begun to demonstrate early signs of volatility. A combination of corporate efforts to position Verses AI for opportunity may be of interest to traders in particular. The current proposed effort to uplist from the OTCQB to the Nasdaq has been accompanied by a 1:9 reverse split. Reverse stock splits are typically viewed as less favorable to existing shareholders, but there are occasional exceptions. An uplisting to the Nasdaq for Verses AI, if it is completed, can be a significant event in the corporation's history. The resulting post-reverse split share count will be an attractive 23.5 million outstanding shares for Verses AI. Timing in the market is important, so the recent reverse stock split and proposed uplisting to the Nasdaq may have improved the fundamentals for new traders considering this stock.

As with most companies, the proof is in the actual execution of a business model to generate the top and bottom-line numbers consistently on a quarterly basis. Verses AI has yet to successfully execute on a business model, as revenues were nil for the latest quarter ending December 31, 2024. Still, the corporate maneuvering by Verses AI management may have merit. The current increase in market awareness that has brought volatility to the stock may be of interest to a new universe of traders on the Nasdaq who may have been less likely to trade an OTC bulletin board stock. Verses AI may also receive some level of new asset allocation from fund managers and institutions who by definition will buy the new Nasdaq listing as it is an AI-related stock. We want to be clear that the proposed uplisting to the Nasdaq is likely, but it has not yet been completed, and there is no guarantee that it will occur.

Gannett Co., Inc.: A Deep Value Turnaround With Hidden Optionality

Mohsen Zafari | Strong Buy

Gannett Co., Inc. (GCI) is in the middle of a turnaround, shifting from a traditional print company to a more profitable digital publisher. Although the real inflection point, where digital revenue surpasses legacy print and commercial revenue with digital growth outpacing the decline in the traditional segment, has not been reached yet, I think there are positive signs showing that they are close and on the right track to eventually get there. The current depressed valuation provides enough margin of safety to invest in Gannett.

Additionally, there are some pretty good options and opportunities that the market currently values at zero. They have a lawsuit against Google which, depending on the outcome, could generate significant additional capital for investors. Furthermore, a new revenue stream from AI companies to use Gannett's content to train AI models is very likely, and their scale gives them a competitive advantage to benefit from this opportunity.

Gannett is a publisher with over 300 digital news and media brands, including USA Today, in the United States. They also own more than 200 news and media brands in the United Kingdom. Even with shrinking revenue, free cash flow has significantly improved and has been positive in the last two years. I think it is reasonable to expect at least $50 million of FCF going forward. This is more than 11% of FCF yield at the current valuation. Additionally, successful turnaround can notably grow FCF.

M&T Bank: Consistent Dividends And Long-Term Growth Potential

Low Budget Dividend Investing | Buy

Last week, in my article evaluating Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) for Seeking Alpha, I mentioned that I have been looking to add another financial holding or two to my portfolio since my attention has been focused on allotting funds to other sectors for a while now. Although I do consider Fifth Third Bank to be an intriguing investment possibility, I ultimately decided to look elsewhere in my search for another financial stock to drop into my basket. Sticking with regional banks, for the time being, I started thinking about some of the banks with whom I have had accounts over the years, which led me to M&T Bank Corporation (MTB). As a former customer who had generally positive experiences with the bank, I decided to take a deeper look at the company's stock, considering the merits of investing in M&T Bank from the perspective of a dividend growth investor.

Founded in Buffalo, New York as the Manufacturers and Traders Trust Company, M&T Bank Corporation has grown into one of the larger regional banking operations in the eastern United States. With total assets of $208 billion, more than 1000 branches, and 22,000 employees, M&T is the fourteenth largest bank in the country, providing customers with retail banking services such as cash management, letters of credit, and lending and leasing; retail banking services including mortgages, checking and savings accounts, credit cards, and personal loans; a suite of wealth management solutions such as trust and estate services; and business banking services such as small business loans.

M&T Bank prides itself on having "been profitable for 170 consecutive quarters - more than 42 straight years," boasting that the institution "is one of only three peer banks to report a profit in every quarter during the Great Recession (2008) and the only peer bank not to cut its dividend through the financial crisis.

TFS Financial Corp.: A Long-Forgotten Second-Step Thrift Conversion Can Still Provide Value

Dan Stringer | Buy

TFS Financial Corporation (TFSL) is a community bank with physical presences in Ohio and Florida. They also serve twenty-seven other states and the District of Columbia through their online operations. The company was founded in 1938 by the Stefanski family, whose son Mark serves as the current CEO of TFSL. It undertook a first step of thrift conversion by issuing 31.7% of its shares to the public in April 2007 with the remaining shares held in a Mutual Holding company.

It is not clear why TFSL undertook the effort to become a stock company. Some of the benefits of converting to the stock company are that it instills a governance structure to watch management; this could well have been a good step to professionalize the bank even while the Stefanski family continues to run it with two of the CEO’s children also now in the management team. Public stock can be a great tool to compensate management employees as well. Insiders do hold 5% of the public float, with CEO Mark Stefanski holding 1.4% of the outstanding public float.

The more common reason to undertake a thrift conversion is to participate in mergers and acquisitions to better monetize the entity than it could by remaining a mutual. The company aggressively bought shares back until 2019, reducing the public float by half and leaving with public shareholders owning 19.1% of the outstanding shares. However, since then, it has seemed to remain in stasis, both from a business perspective and in its share trading range.

The company is now 18 years on from this initial first step; most other companies go through the second step in three to five years. At this point, the motivation is as a tool to help incentivize management by providing a solid income vehicle.

The Other Five Fit For Mention

Aya Gold & Silver: Excessive Selloff Strikes Before 3X Production Surge

Laurentian Research | Strong Buy

Aya Gold & Silver Inc.'s (AYASF)(AYA:CA) stock declined due to a $27.35 million impairment resultant from its abandonment of the Tijirit project, Mauritania, and high Q4 2024 cash costs at Zgounder. Despite setbacks, Aya's Zgounder mine is expected to significantly reduce cash costs and triple silver production in 2025, boosting cash margins. Aya's strong near-term catalysts include increased mill throughput, higher ore grades, improved recoveries, and a favorable silver market, positioning it for substantial growth. The recent selloff presents a buying opportunity, as Aya is undervalued relative to peers and poised for a threefold production ramp-up and cost reductions in 2025.

Sellas Life Sciences: A Hold For Now With Huge, But Uncertain, Near-Term Catalyst

IB's Thoughts | Hold

Sellas Life Sciences Group, Inc.'s (SLS) stock has dropped significantly, with a Hold rating due to uncertainty around the final Phase 3 trial data for GPS in AML. Interim results for GPS are promising but inconclusive; the trial continues until 80 patients die, creating investment uncertainty and ongoing cash burn. The company raised $25M in January 2025, extending its runway to late 2025, but may need more funds for regulatory and commercial plans. Current SLS shareholders should hold steady and consider accumulating if the stock dips below $1/share, given the potential for exponential upside if GPS succeeds.

Surge Energy: High FCF Yield Makes The Stock Finally Cheap Enough

Andrew Mach | Buy

Surge Energy Inc. (SGY:CA)(ZPTAF) offers a compelling setup with a clean balance sheet, high FCF yield, and no cash taxes expected until 2030. The company remains solid with high-quality assets trading at a depressed valuation. At US$65 WTI, Surge generates a strong 16% FCF yield and a dividend yield of over 8%. Despite its high dividend yield, Surge is not suitable for retirement portfolios due to the risk of dividend cuts in lower oil price scenarios. The CEO, Paul Colborne, runs a highly profitable company with great assets. Yet, the shares are trading at a depressed valuation, with price trading close to their PDP (Proved, Developed, Producing) reserves.

Gold Fields: No Longer A Spectator In The Gold Rush

Acorn Accumulator Analytics | Buy

Gold Fields Limited (GFI) (OTCPK:GFIOF) is a diversified gold producer. GFI was historically a South African mining giant, but nearly half of its production now comes from Australia. In 2013, Gold Fields spun off three of its older South African mines to Sibanye Gold (now Sibanye Stillwater (SBSW)), allowing GFI to focus on more profitable, lower-cost mines elsewhere. GFI experienced a difficult first half of 2024, characterized by operational challenges, severe weather conditions, and underperformance by two of its four core assets (St Ives and South Deep). Despite a challenging first half, GFI improved in H2, with production up 26% and costs down 12%. Management's guidance suggests a 13.45% production increase in 2025 at a 3.31% lower All-In-Sustaining-Cost, supporting a conditional "Buy" rating.

Nextdoor Stock Is Ready For Major Platform Changes

Value Detection | Hold

Nextdoor Holdings, Inc. (KIND) is a social network that offers hyper-local interactions between neighbors. The business has gone through lots of platform revisions and trials under the leadership of Sarah Friar. However, the market clearly didn’t like her performance, and the stock price has been depressed for a long time. Last year, the board brought back co-founder Nirav Tolia who is known for his startup mentality. Now, it seems like the management has the urgency to make big transformations, and big things may happen in Nextdoor during the next 12–18 months. I think this stock is worth close monitoring moving forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Finding Shelter In Morgan Stanley's High Yielding Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

I don’t think Morgan Stanley (MS) needs a lengthy introduction as it's a household name in the financial services industry. The company generates a very substantial portion of its net revenue in its asset management division. But with almost $7B in investment banking fees in 2023, it's understandable why investors still look at Morgan Stanley as an investment bank.

I have been keeping an eye on the preferred shares that have been issued by Morgan Stanley, and given the strong performance of the investment bank and the excellent preferred dividend coverage ratio, I still think Morgan Stanley’s preferred equity isn’t a bad place to park some cash.

The preferred dividends remain very well covered

It goes without saying one of the first and more important elements of discussing Morgan Stanley’s preferred shares is to figure out how well the preferred dividends are covered.

During 2024, the company reported total revenue of $61.8B, including a net interest income of approximately $8.6B. But as the image below shows, it’s mainly the investment banking and trading revenue that really helped push the total reported revenue higher by in excess of 10% to the aforementioned $61.8B. On top of that, the asset management division also showed a 15% revenue increase.

And although the net revenue increased by a double-digit percentage, the total increase of the non-interest expenses was very manageable at just over $2B (representing an increase of about 5% compared to a year ago). And it’s perhaps also interesting and important to note that the total amount of provisions for credit losses decreased by approximately 50% on a year-over-year basis. And although that $268M reduction is relatively negligible in the greater scheme of things, it does indicate Morgan Stanley has its risks under control.

Taking all the elements mentioned above into consideration, Morgan Stanley saw its pre-tax income increase by almost 50%, resulting in a net profit of $13.53 compared to just $9.2M in FY 2023. Approximately $139M of the reported net profit was attributable to non-controlling interests, resulting in $13.39B in net income attributable to Morgan Stanley. About 4.4% of that attributable net profit was used to cover the preferred share dividends, which means the net income attributable to common shareholders of Morgan Stanley was approximately $12.8B, resulting in an EPS of $8.04. That’s a sharp increase compared to the same period last year, and the per-share result was boosted by the continuous reduction in its share count.

But going back to what matters from the perspective of a preferred share investor: The preferred dividends are well-covered as Morgan Stanley only needed 1/20 th of its attributable net profit to cover the preferred dividends.

And looking at the balance sheet (shown above), it's also quite clear the total ratio of preferred equity vs. common equity is quite advantageous for the preferred equity holders. As the image above shows, the balance sheet contains approximately $105B in equity, including $9.75B in preferred equity. This means there's in excess of $95B in common equity, which ranks junior to the preferred equity.

Another look at the Series E preferred shares

In my previous articles, I kept an eye on the Series E preferred shares issued by Morgan Stanley. These preferred shares are trading with (MS.PR.E) as the ticker symbol. And although this was originally a fix-to-float issue, when the LIBOR benchmark rate was phased out due to the acceptance of the SOFR as new and supposedly more reliable benchmark, Morgan Stanley elected to keep the preferred dividend fixed at the same rate as the initial period rather than letting the preferred dividend float based on the SOFR benchmark. The stock pays a dividend of approximately 7.125% (there are some minimal quarterly fluctuations, but the recent quarterly dividend was $0.4453 per preferred share.

Keep in mind these preferred shares have been callable since October 2023 and can still be called at any time, subject to a 30-day notice period. That’s why you shouldn’t overpay for this security given the call risk.

Investment thesis

Trading at $25.13 per share, the Series E preferred shares issued by Morgan Stanley currently offer a preferred dividend yield of just under 7.1%. Given the robust earnings profile of Morgan Stanley, I'm confident the investment bank will continue to be able to cover its commitments despite this preferred share being a non-cumulative issue. The reputational damage to skip preferred dividends would be far worse than saving the less than $150M per quarter on preferred dividends.

I have a small long position in Morgan Stanley’s Series E preferred shares, and now the share price is trading closer to the principal value of $25/share, I may add to this position during these turbulent times.

data.title: Markets Watch Out For Core CPI With Big Banks Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

As Trump’s announcement this week on reciprocal tariffs rattled Wall Street, markets will keep a close eye on upcoming economic events. Investors are getting increasingly concerned about the potential inflationary pressures from Trump’s tariffs and their impact on economic activity. Important economic reports such as the Employment Trends Index and Consumer Credit are due to be announced on Monday, with NFIB Small Business Optimism to be announced on Tuesday. Meanwhile, Wednesday will include Wholesale inventories, and minutes from the last FOMC meeting, with Jobless Claims and Core CPI set to be published on Thursday.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), Morgan Stanley (MS), BlackRock (BLK), and Levi Strauss (LEVI) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, April 7: Levi Strauss and Dave & Buster’s Entertainment (PLAY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, April 8: Tilray Brands (TLRY) and RPM International (RPM). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, April 9: Constellation Brands (STZ) and BBB Foods (TBBB). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, April 10: CarMax (KMX). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, April 11: JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo, Morgan Stanley, BlackRock, and Unity Bancorp (UNTY). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Super Micro Computer (SMCI) and Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. Semler Scientific (SMLR) and Quantum Corporation (QMCO) have an elevated level of short interest outstanding on them heading into the new week. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Maywood Acquisition (MAYA), ProAssurance (PRA), and Radius Recycling (RDUS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Xerox (XRX) and Vaxcyte (PCVX). Newsmax (NMAX) will also be watched closely after a wild first week of trading.

IPO watch: The lockup periods for KinderCare (KLC), Click Holdings (CLIK), and SKK Holdings (SKK) are set to expire on Monday, while lockup periods for Upstream Bio (UPB), CeriBell (CBLL), Camp4 Therapeutics (CAMP), and Star Fashion Culture (STFS) will expire on Wednesday. Meanwhile, FatPipe (FATN) is expected to price its IPO on Monday, and begin trading on Tuesday.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include McCormick (MKC), Quest Diagnostics (DGX), Dollar General (DG), Mastercard (MA), Gap (GAP), Oracle (ORCL), Salesforce (CRM), and AT&T (T). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include Cal-Maine (CALM) from $1.489 to $2.325, H.B. Fuller (FUL) from $0.22 to $0.24, Constellation Brands (STZ) from $1.01 to $1.08, Costco (COST) from $1.16 to $1.20 and Tanger (SKT) from $0.27 to $0.28. Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: The NAB Show in Las Vegas will continue on Monday after starting over the weekend. The two-day Stifel Virtual Targeted Oncology Forum will begin on Tuesday with participation from Compugen (CGEN), Zymeworks (ZYME), and Innate Pharma (IPHA). The Paris Blockchain Week will also begin on Tuesday, while the EIA is set to publish its short-term energy outlook that day. The three-day Google Cloud Next event will begin in Las Vegas on Wednesday.

Morgan Stanley Earnings Preview: Morgan Stanley is set to announce its first quarter earnings results on Friday, with analysts expecting EPS of $2.30 on revenue of $16.79B. Barclays expects investment banking activity to be impacted by recent uncertainty in the markets. Still, compared to last year, overall Institutional Securities Group revenue should increase with strength in trading outweighing softness in investment banking, Barclays said in a note. “Wealth Management and Investment Management should also act as a ballast aided by stabilization in net interest income trends,” the analysts added.

JPMorgan Earnings Preview: JPMorgan is scheduled to report its first quarter results on Friday. Analysts expect the banking giant to report earnings per share of $4.62 on a revenue of $43.88B. Barclays expects EPS to come above consensus, driven by a lower fee income and higher provision forecast. Net interest income should be modestly lower in the quarter due to day count and balance sheet contraction, according to analysts. “In addition, results should be impacted by seasonally higher trading activity while asset quality continues to normalize,” Barclays analysts wrote in a note.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sweetgreen: Tough Set Up For The Rest Of 2025 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Sweetgreen (NYSE:SG) previously with a buy rating, as I expected a macro recovery to drive revenue growth back to high-teen percentages. I was wrong. The macro did not turn for the better; instead, it got worse, and I now believe it is going to be a tough year ahead for SG. With slowing demand, limited pricing power, and margin pressure, I think there are few reasons to stay optimistic in the near term. Therefore, I downgraded my rating from buy to hold.

Macroeconomic Risks Heavily Impact Demand Recovery

The 2nd of April 2025 was a game changer. President Trump’s tariffs were a lot worse than I expected, and I think there are going to be huge repercussions for the US macroeconomy and, ultimately, the consumers. For a consumer-facing brand like SG, this is a tough pill to swallow. Importantly, I don’t think this is the end of this tariff event. We have yet to see the reciprocal tariffs from other nations like Europe and China and how Trump will react to them. For all we know, this leads to an all-out trade war that reignites inflation—at a time when the Fed is pulling levers to keep it under control.

For SG, I don’t see how it will be able to get out of this unscathed. Higher import costs are likely to squeeze food costs, which SG has to either bear the cost increase or pass through to consumers. For the latter, I think it will be an extremely difficult job since consumers spending power is going to be heavily impacted as well. My view is that if inflation rebounds, discretionary spending (like eating out) will be one of the first categories that US households will cut. And for SG, which depends heavily on urban, discretionary dining behavior, I think the outlook is looking really weak.

Soft Comp Guide and Traffic Trends Signal Weak Demand Setup

I also don’t expect much upside surprise here, since management themselves are already putting out very soft guidance. Here are the numbers for reference: FY25 same-store sales [SSS] are only guided to grow just 2–4% (3% at the midpoint), which is a relatively steep step-down from 5.5% in FY24, and I think this guide has room for downside. Note that the earnings call was held on 26th February, and at that time, nobody knew the real impact of Trump’s tariffs. Also, I think it is unlikely that SG will be able to exert the same level of pricing power it did in FY24. Of the 5.5% y/y SSS growth in FY24, 4.4% of it was from pricing, so traffic really only grew by 1.2%. If we assume the entire pricing power is taken off the table (which will have some impact in 1H25 due to the pricing roll-off impact) plus the fact that traffic likely turns negative in FY25, FY25 SSS growth could be negative.

Margin pressure

The problem gets worse when I look at the cost structure of SG. As of FY24, SG had G&A expenses of ~$110 million in FY24, which equates to roughly 16.4% of revenue, and in Q4'24 G&A actually rose as a percentage of revenue vs. Q4'23 (from 17.1% to 17.6%). This is not a problem if SG is able to accelerate its revenue growth as I thought previously, but now that demand is very likely to slow or turn negative, I think it will be extremely tough for SG to see any meaningful margin expansion. This matters more since SG is still in operating losses, which means the timeline to reaching breakeven is likely pushed out, and I expect this to weigh heavily on the stock sentiment—especially since investors are likely to be risk-averse and prefer to invest in profitable businesses (my own view).

Near-term bull case

That said, if I were to play devil’s advocate, here are two reasons why I think there is a bull case angle for the near term. As much as I am negative about SG’s ability to raise prices again in FY25, it is a fact that they managed to roll out a low double-digit price hike late in the quarter, and traffic was not noted to have declined (Q4'24 traffic growth is positive). This could mean that SG has actually been underpricing its product relative to the value customers’ perceive, and there could still be headroom to increase pricing. I am not confident of this, but if this is true, SG could easily meet its SSS growth guidance.

The second reason is the full SG Rewards that is expected to relaunch in Q2'25 with a 10-points-per-dollar structure. The financial impact is positive at both the top and cost line. At the top line, this relaunch may help SG drive better frequency and stickiness, especially at a time when consumers would want to look for “cost-saving deals.” At the cost line, this could drive customer acquisition cost downward (depending on how successful it is) and thus lift the pressure on margins.

Valuation

If SG cannot accelerate its revenue growth, then the equity story and share price upside are no longer attractive. Management guided for ~14% y/y revenue growth in FY25, and although I think there is a good chance SG may miss this guide, I will use it for modeling purposes. Assuming the same revenue growth trend persists into FY26, as the macroeconomy will take time to recover, SG will only generate $878 million in revenue, per my model. With this level of growth, SG is very unlikely to see any form of meaningful multiple upgrade. SG now trades at 3.4x forward revenue, and I think the market will likely derate this back to at least 3x since there is no reason for SG to trade above its historical average (which is 3x).

Conclusion

I give a neutral rating for SG. I think the near-term macro headwinds are just too significant to ignore. SG’s growth is decelerating, cost pressures are still elevated, and the equity story is a lot shakier given the uncertain growth outlook. If SSS growth turns negative or the breakeven timeline is pushed further out, I see a real risk of more multiple compressions. Until there’s more clarity, I prefer to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: B2Gold Is One Of The Cheapest Gold Mining Companies With A Near Term Catalyst
data.description:
Introduction

The surge in gold prices in recent quarters has left many investors in awe. Many investors are now looking into gold mining companies that often have an operational leverage effect on their bottom line with rising gold prices. One of the gold miners is B2Gold (NYSE:BTG). Among its gold mining peers, BTG has been one of the worst-performing stocks in the last 12 months and is among the cheapest of the lot. However, I believe this should change in 2025 with a ramp-up in production as well as its low valuation. I believe the company has a decent upside from the current price point. Moreover, surging gold prices should further benefit the company.

Not so good year gone by

B2G reported its year-end results earlier in February; the result was a miss on the bottom line, while the topline remained largely in line with the moderated street’s expectation. The Q4'24 results marked the third consecutive negative EPS surprise, largely led by operational issues in one of its largest mining complexes (Fekola).

To put it into perspective, Fekola generates 50% of the overall production for B2Gold. During the start of 2024, BTG management gave a production guidance of 860-940K oz for 2024, which would have marked a ~12-19% YoY decline in production. The initial guidance was lower than 2023, primarily due to a delay in receiving an exploitation license for Fekola Regional from the government of Mali, which delayed 80-100K oz of gold that were scheduled to be mined in 2024.

Subsequently, during the Q2'24 earnings call, management announced more bad news for the company. One of the excavators was lost at the Fekola site due to an operator error, due to which management revised 2024 projections even lower by 50K oz. The Q3'24 production was even weaker than expected due to a delay in the replacement of an excavator and guidance was even lowered to 800-870K oz for the year. Finally, the production came out to be ~804K oz for 2024, which was near the lower range of the guidance primarily due to the reasons mentioned above and a strike in the Fekola mines.

BTG should witness strong growth in 2025

While 2024 was a tough year for BTG, 2025 is going to be the opposite of that. BTG’s management anticipates gold production for 2025 to be in the range of 970-1075K oz, which is ~20-33% higher than the production in 2024. This should be primarily driven by the reversal of several headwinds faced in 2024 and the much anticipated commencement of the Goose project.

The output from the Fekola mines is expected to be considerably higher this year, as last year the company faced incidents like excavator breakdown and strike, which halted some of the production. Now with equipment in place, the production of high-grade ore from Fekola phase 7 and cardinal pits has been started since the start of 2025 and should add to a significant increase in production this year.

In addition to this, after delays in receiving the exploitation license from the government of Mali in 2024 for the Fekola Regional, the company has finally reached terms with the Mali government on September 2024, which can result in potential 80-100K oz gold production in 2025 itself, which could ramp up to 180K oz per year in the subsequent years.

Another key growth driver for BTG for 2025 should be the potential commencement of the Goose Project. BTG has been working on this asset since 2013 and finally, in the second quarter of 2025, the first gold pour is expected, with ramp-up in subsequent quarters. The project is estimated to have a capital cost of ~C$1.54 billion or $1.1 billion with a 2.44 million oz deposit. At the current price of ~$3100 an oz, this deposit should be equivalent to ~$8 billion. As per management’s estimates, this site should generate ~120-150K oz in 2025 and 310K oz annually in the subsequent years till 2031.

BTG has several other projects underway, like the Gramalote project with the potential to generate ~234K oz a year for the first 5 years and the Antelope deposit that has the potential to produce ~65K oz a year. However, these projects are still under development and production can only begin by the late 2020s. Meanwhile, the ramp-up from the Fekola and Goose mines should sustain growth in the near to medium term.

Due to the anticipated potential of BTG, the Wall Street analysts are also pretty bullish on the stock. The stock has an average price target of ~$3.68, with some analysts also projecting as high as $4.5, while some are projecting a potential downside of ~$2.3.

Valuation

BTG is one of the cheapest gold mining companies with a Seeking Alpha Quant valuation rating of A. The company has a non-GAAP forward PE ratio of 7.14x (the lowest among its peers) and a price-to-book ratio of ~1.3x, which is also near the lower end when compared to peers.

Moreover, given the rising gold prices, gold miners should benefit immensely going forward. In times of rising gold prices, the cost structure of gold miners tends to rise at a consistent pace while the gold prices outpace the growth in the cost of production. This dynamic creates a situation of operating leverage that tends to increase the bottom line at a much faster rate than the rate of growth in gold prices.

Risk to consider before buying B2Gold

BTG is a gold mining company with its mining assets in different parts of the world. However, much of BTG’s production and profits are associated with the Fekola mining complex. An investor in the stock of BTG should consider the regulatory risk associated with this mining complex, like the new mining code that the government of Mali enacted in 2023, which increased taxes for new projects. Regulatory changes like these could have a major impact on BTG’s financial condition, especially given its concentration in the mining complex.

Moreover, as mentioned earlier, rising gold prices have a multiplier effect on the EPS of gold miners; the opposite is also true. A sudden and deep fall in gold prices can also impact the bottom line severely.

Conclusion

Given the near-term tailwinds, cheap valuation and tailwind from rising gold prices, I believe BTG is a bargain at current prices. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Widows And Orphans Income Investments Yielding 6%
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

I love the terminology of a “widow and orphan investment” mainly because the focus here is on extreme levels of safety, extremely low risk, and hands-off types of investments. These are investments that you can buy and hold, and not have to babysit or monitor, and simply benefit from over the long run. The idea here is that if a husband or wife passes away, or both parents pass away, leaving an orphan behind, this widow, widower, or orphan can simply have these investments and benefit from them without having to be actively engaged in the investment process.

For something to be a widow and orphan investment, it must be, by definition, exceptionally low risk. From my perspective, a widow and orphan income investment must also provide a livable yield that does not require active management—AKA selling shares—to be able to benefit from. If I've got a 4-year-old orphan sitting in front of me who needs to have an income stream to cover and provide for their meals, then the investments better not rely on selling shares. Instead, those investments should be paying something.

I want to look at a few different widows and orphan quality preferred securities. Preferred securities are less risky than common equity, often provide a better yield than common equity with lower volatility, and are more hands-off. If you own a solid preferred security, it will continue to pay you, regardless of what's going on in the world around you. You don't need to sit there and babysit it or time the market; you can simply collect the income from it. Additionally, a big must for these investments is the low chance of a call. When it comes to preferred securities, the company has the option to call the security, meaning that all of its shares are redeemed at par, usually $25, and when that is exercised, you don't have an option to avoid it.

So today, the preferred securities I'm sharing have exceptionally low call risk as well as high coverage.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: AGM Preferred – Yield 6.4%

Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corporation (AGM) is better known colloquially as Farmer Mac. Their common shares have performed exceptionally well over the decades, all while providing a steadily growing dividend:

AGM provides a massive level of liquidity to the agricultural loan market, which could be considered a niche market. It is a very busy one considering that farmers will need to get loans to buy equipment for running their operations, as well as people who are moving from the city to rural areas will get loans from companies originating in rural areas. AGM is able to issue shares or debt to buy these loans from financial institutions that originate them and maintain them. They play an essential role in this niche sector of the loan market, much like Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac provide essential liquidity to the mortgage sector.

Recently, AGM has focused on utility and infrastructure-focused lending. This is designed to help get more infrastructure developed for renewable energy, as well as for connectivity with rural homes needing connectivity through fiber or other infrastructure needs. AGM is extremely meticulous about its goals for profitability on its lending, as well as its capital efficiency and turning over capital. This means that they are not slow to call or replace preferred securities or debt when they become no longer attractive to maintain. Overall, AGM would be a company that I would consider to have a higher call risk on preferred securities that have a higher yield at PAR.

Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corp. 4.875% Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series G (AGM.PR.G) yields 6.4% currently and pays a qualified dividend to shareholders. The low dividend rate means its susceptibility to a call is very low. All of AGM's other preferreds currently offer a similar current yield but carry a higher PAR yield, meaning this will be the lowest priority preferred to be called by management. AGM covers their preferred dividends by 7.7x from net income in 2024, and this is expected to continue to improve as they redeemed their Series C preferred mid-year.

Overall, AGM-G is an excellent widow- and orphans-style preferred, offering a highly attractive yield.

Pick #2: RF Preferred – 6.5% Yield

Regions Financial Corporation (RF) is a large regional bank focused on the Southeastern United States. RF has been very successful historically in offering low interest on their accounts while collecting strong interest on their loans. This has continued to grow as their focus over time. They've not had to offer as high interest as their peers on deposits and continue to lend strongly. Their most recent quarterly earnings report underwhelmed the market concerning their projected growth in 2025, which caused a sell-off in their common shares. Any growth, even slow for the market's tastes, will continue to improve preferred coverage.

RF is also very willing to remove expensive debt and replace it. Near the end of 2024, RF called what was going to be a floating-rate security and replaced it with a preferred at a fixed rate of 6.95% (RF.PR.F). RF has no further pressing fixed-to-floating rate preferreds until 2029, when their series C (RF.PR.C) begins floating. Overall, their lowest at PAR yielding fixed rate preferred offering is the most attractive for a long-term holding:

  • Regions Financial Corp., 4.45% Dep Shares Series E Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock (RF.PR.E) yields 6.5%.

RF's preferred coverage is 15.9x from Net Income. By buying the Series E preferred, we can once again insulate ourselves from call risk because, at current rates, RF could only get a near 7% yield for a newly issued fixed-rate security. I do not expect there to be a nearly 300-basis-point cut or 400-basis-point cut in the near future to interest rates. You may see additional cuts to bring us back to a more neutral level, but this preferred security having a 4.45% yield at PAR makes it highly unlikely that RF will call it or desire to call it.

Overall, RF-E offers a very low-risk 6.3% yield that is a qualified dividend and highly unlikely to ever be called.

Conclusion

A widow- and orphan-style income investment will continue to pay a strong dividend for decades upon decades, with little or no portfolio management or governance needed. This means that the widow or orphan can have this purchased for them and never have to look at it again, for the most part. To achieve this, we must accept that there are limitations to any sort of widow and orphan investment. Usually, they will not have market-matching or market-beating returns. If you're investing in a preferred security, you're giving up potential upside for a guaranteed income. Both of these options highlighted today are fixed-rate investments, meaning the yield you get when you purchase them is the yield you're going to collect going forward. There is no variability there. This also gives you a high level of visibility into the income you'll receive. If you were to invest $1,000,000 into either one of these today, you would be collecting over $60,000 a year. It would not shift or change year to year; it would be set.

The benefit of having investments like these in your portfolio is that they reduce the amount of workload that your portfolio demands. I recommend having no less than 42 different unique income investments in your portfolio—Our Rule of 42—and the more widow- and orphan-style ones that you can include, the less portfolio management you are going to have to undertake year to year, allowing you to have more free time to do what you love.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Beware Of Some Red Flags Before You Invest In The Recovery Of Superior Plus
data.description:
Superior Plus (OTCPK:SUUIF) has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500 in the last 12 months. The stock has declined 31% whereas the index has gained 3%. The picture is even worse if one examines the last five years or the last decade. After such a vast underperformance, it is only natural that some investors may conclude that Superior Plus has become ripe to cover the lost ground. However, investors who decide to invest in the recovery of the company should be aware of some red flags.

Business overview

Superior Plus is a leading distributor and marketer of propane and other products in the U.S. and Canada, serving approximately 750,000 customer locations via its more than 2,100 delivery vehicles.

Source: Investor Presentation

The company is the third-largest distributor of propane in North America and the largest distributor of propane in Canada. It is also the largest provider of compressed natural gas across North America. As a result, it enjoys significant economies of scale.

Moreover, Superior Plus has decent growth prospects. The consumption of propane in North America is expected to remain flat over the next five years but there is significant potential for market share gains.

Source: Investor Presentation

Superior Plus has a 6% market share in propane while large-scale competitors have a 23% market share in total. Thanks to the highly fragmented status of the market, there is ample room for consolidation. In addition, the consumption of compressed natural gas is expected to grow by 9% per year on average until 2030. This is likely to prove a material growth driver for Superior Plus. Management has provided guidance for 25% growth of adjusted EBITDA over the next three years.

On the other hand, the company has some red flags. In its latest earnings report, management boasted of having achieved 10% growth in adjusted EBITDA in 2024 vs. 2023. However, the company switched from a profit per share of $0.17 in 2023 to a loss per share of -$0.15 in 2024. Management attributed the poor performance to abnormally warm winter weather and challenging conditions in the business of compressed natural gas. The above performance shows that Superior Plus may grow its earnings per share modestly or negligibly over the next three years even if it meets its guidance for 25% growth of adjusted EBITDA.

The high sensitivity of the results of Superior Plus to the prevailing weather conditions is easy to understand. Unfortunately, this sensitivity has resulted in an extremely volatile performance record. To be sure, the company has posted losses in 4 of the last 10 years and has failed to grow its earnings over the last decade. Even in its last profitable year, which was 2023, Superior Plus posted earnings per share of $0.17, which were just 13% higher than the earnings per share of $0.15 in 2015. The absence of a trend that shows long-term growth of earnings is a red flag.

Another red flag is the focus of management on EBITDA instead of earnings. This is evident in the earnings reports and presentations of Superior Plus. When a company puts emphasis on EBITDA, it usually signals that it is far from establishing a reliable trend of long-term growth of earnings. Indeed, legendary investor Warren Buffett has stated: “When a company focuses on EBITDA, it tries to dress up its financial statements”. The combination of a markedly poor and volatile performance record and a management focused on EBITDA greatly increases the odds of poor long-term returns in the future.

Debt

Superior Plus carries a material amount of debt on its balance sheet. Its net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) is currently standing at $2.2 billion. This amount is nearly double the market capitalization of the stock and hence it is excessive. In addition, interest expense consumes 52% of operating expenses.

The leverage ratio (Net debt to EBITDA) increased from 3.9x at the end of 2023 to 4.1x now. Management stated that it aims to reduce the leverage ratio towards 3.0x over the medium term. However, until management delivers its goal, high-interest expense will continue exerting a drag on the bottom line of the company.

All the above facts do not mean that Superior Plus will face liquidity issues anytime soon. On the other hand, they render the company vulnerable to a potential downturn in its business. Even if the company never faces liquidity issues, the high debt load is a testament to its weak business model.

Underperformance

As mentioned above, Superior Plus has exhibited a highly volatile performance record, with losses in many years and no long-term growth trend in place. As a result, the stock has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500 in almost any time horizon one can examine. During the last decade, the stock has plunged 56% whereas the S&P 500 has rallied 156%. During the last five years, the stock has declined 10% whereas the S&P 500 has rallied 116%.

When a stock underperforms the broad market over the long run, it usually signals the absence of a reliable, strong business model in place. In other words, the long-term underperformance does not bode well for the ability of the company to create shareholder value.

Upside risk

If next winter proves abnormally cold, it will probably form a strong tailwind to the results of Superior Plus. Even better, in such a scenario, the company may use its rich free cash flows to reduce its debt load, and thus it may reduce its interest expense. Lower interest expense will provide an additional growth driver for earnings. Overall, if next winter is favorable for Superior Plus, the stock is likely to enjoy a strong relief rally.

However, basing an investing thesis on such unforeseeable outcomes is highly risky. Superior Plus may be suitable for traders with a short-term horizon from time to time, but it is risky for investors with a long-term perspective.

Final thoughts

Superior Plus has underperformed the S&P 500 by a wide margin in almost any time horizon one can examine. This does not mean that the stock has become attractive. The company has decent growth prospects in the next three years, but investors should be aware of some red flags before investing in the recovery of the stock. The poor performance record, the absence of a reliable future growth trajectory, the focus of management on EBITDA and a weak balance sheet are all indicative of a weak business model, which will probably prime the stock for continued underperformance over the long run.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rivian And Its Broken Risk-To-Reward
data.description:
My Thesis

From what I see in Rivian Automotive's (NASDAQ:RIVN) investment case, the firm is expanding quite rapidly indeed, but looking at the industry's specifics and the company's current business cycle, I think buying the stock today presents a relatively unfavorable risk-reward profile. That's why, in my opinion, the most appropriate rating for Rivian as of today is “Hold”, not “Buy”.

My Reasoning

Rivian's Q4 numbers surprised the market, with the firm turning its first-ever positive gross profit as RIVN achieved a $31,000 reduction in the cost of goods sold per vehicle versus the prior year's quarter. Thanks to the GP breakeven, RIVN's adjusted EBITDA losses narrowed quite meaningfully to ~$277 million—“the best performance since production began and better than analyst expectations,” Goldman Sachs notes in its post-earnings note to clients (proprietary source, February 2025). That's why the firm beat the bottom-line consensus so massively:

The top line was also much more than expected by the consensus as their revenue-per-unit also increased, which was aided by “a richer mix including the popular Tri-Motor configuration and nearly $300 million in regulatory credit sales during the quarter.” The regulatory credit sales likely explain the amount of the revenue beat for Q4, but it looks like a one-off thing that we can't really incorporate into our forecasting.

Another important corporate news that we got from Q4 was Rivian's decision to split the operation segments into “Automotive” and “Software and Services” so going forward, investors will have better earnings/margins visibility. Speaking about the margins: The Automotive segment, the key growth driver, is currently yielding ~7% in gross profit margin. The Software and Services segment, which is about “remarketing, service, charging, subscriptions”, and crucially, “revenue from the Volkswagen Group joint venture (JV)”, posted ~$214 million in sales with a strong 28% gross margin. They're going to recognize ~$2 billion over the next 4 years. As the fourth quarter was the first period to demonstrate tangible financial benefits from the partnership, I believe we can confidently incorporate the remaining revenue into our estimates for future periods.

After receiving ~$1.3 billion from the VW JV closing in November 2024, the firm closed Q4 with ~$7.7 billion in cash, so it's a great thing for progressing on their new product launches. We can see that they're actively preparing the existing facility in Normal, Illinois, for the launch of the R2 platform, planning to deliver 46,000-51,000 vehicles for FY2025. But at that midpoint (48,500), it's going to be lower than last year (and well below Goldman's estimate of 55,500):

The Q1 FY2025 is going to be tough because of a “challenging demand environment” as the management commented. The deliveries should be ~8,000 at the mid-point. Although the Q1 production should be ~14,000, they're intentionally building up their inventory levels ahead of a planned month-long factory shutdown in 2H 2025. Despite that, the management sees a positive GP for the full year, which is likely to come from the Software and Services segment's projected $1 billion in sales at ~30% margins. The adjusted EBITDA will stay negative in FY2025 and reach ~$1.8 billion in loss (the midpoint), per Claire McDonough's comments on the call. So ignoring seasonality factors here, the improvement in Q4 isn't going to roll over to this year, unfortunately for those who hoped for a steady path to adj. EBITDA breakeven.

Based on what I see, it looks like the rising expectations of Rivian's breakeven in 2 years (on the adjusted EBITDA basis) look too good to be true. Yes, the Software and Services segment's contribution is going to help them keep pushing the GP higher. I'm uncertain whether the Automotive segment's gross profit will maintain its fourth-quarter growth rate. That's why I'd cautiously assume that the current consensus EBITDA projections will likely be adjusted downward, mirroring the EPS revisions over the past few months.

Rivian is supposed to be a “high-growth stock” in a high-growth EV market, but it looks like the steadily rising competition in the EV market is eating away at its profitable expansion opportunity. I mean, the recent trends in the company's deliveries don't show a company in its “high growth” mode as the EV industry's growth rates started to decelerate with many auto players scaling back their EV expansion plans looking at the changing consumers' needs.

It's tough to be in Rivian's shoes, I guess. I like the management's strategic moves regarding reporting segment shifting, the higher focus on tech-driven services line Connect+, etc. Also, the recently announced spin-off of the micromobility division into a separate company (Also Inc.) is a move in the right direction, which is likely to free up more capital and focus attention on the core R1, R2, and commercial vehicle business. However, the business cycle of the firm suggests its stock price is likely to suffer further. With heavy operating losses amid lowering delivery expectations for at least 1H 2025, Rivian's valuation is simply unsustainably high today.

Talking about valuation, I don't take the negative EV/EBITDA and P/E figures, saying that's the reason for RIVN's overvaluation. It's logical—and everyone knows—that Rivian's key valuation multiples are negative.

But the forwarding EV/Sales ratio should drop by another 50-70% to match those of legacy automakers. I'm not taking Tesla (TSLA) for comparison here because its valuation isn't really dependent on the success of its operations (as history has shown multiple times in the past). But even taking NIO (NIO) for our simple COMPs analysis, we can see that RIVN is valued 3.7x higher. For context: NIO is also a loss-making premium automaker with plenty of cash on the balance sheet, and its Chinese origin doesn't seem to justify such a large discrepancy with RIVN.

Your Takeaway

I'd like to emphasize here again that I actually like what Rivian is doing: their pretty-looking cars are likely to find their place on the market, while the recent corporate changes—better segment breakdown, new tech launches, VG JV monetization, and Also Inc. spin-off—speak of a well-rounded approach for growth. But unfortunately, Rivian exists in an unfavorable business cycle amid a deceleration in its key end-market flood with peers. This creates an unfavorable, kind of broken risk-to-reward setup for those who are willing to buy RIVN on its prolonged dip. I think better times for that will come—for now, Rivian is a “Hold” for me.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Ginebra San Miguel: Strong 2024 Financial Performance And An Unintended Government-Provided Moat
data.description:
Introduction

Ginebra San Miguel, Inc. (GBSMF), one of the leading alcohol beverage companies in the Philippines and the biggest in the gin category, just released its 2024 full-year earnings on March 17, 2025, Philippine Time.

For the year, the company continues to demonstrate robust operating and financial performance, which I will discuss in further detail below, and based on my further analysis of the competitive landscape, it will likely continue to perform for years to come. This article will serve as a follow-up to my first one, where I laid out my investment thesis. Aside from Ginebra's 2024 financial performance, I delve into the Philippine Sin Tax Law that was signed into law by then-President Rodrigo Duterte. While the bill looks to be a headwind to the industry as it discourages consumers from consuming more alcohol, I believe that this regulation actually benefits Ginebra due to its economy of scale that can deter smaller entrants due to the high fixed cost associated with the industry.

With a 2024 operating profit of ₱8.6B and an enterprise value of ₱71.33B, this translates to around 12% earnings yield or an 8.3x EV/EBIT, which is a good deal considering you are buying a market leader in the Philippine spirit market with favorable economics due to regulation. Thus, I will maintain a 'Buy' rating for this stock.

2024 Full-Year Earnings Takeaway

For the full year, Ginebra generated revenue of ₱62.50B, an operating profit of around ₱8.6B, and a net income of ₱7.25B, an increase of 17%, 26%, and 3%, respectively, when compared to the prior year.

Ginebra's Operating margin expanded from 12.73% in 2023 to 13.75% in 2024, while its net margin declined from 13.14% to 11.61% in the same period. The decline in net margin can be attributed to the one-time gain from the sale of Don Papa. Without the one-time gain from Don Papa, the net margin would have expanded from 10.98% to 11.61% and would have increased by 23.21% from ₱5.89B to ₱7.26B.

For its future growth, Ginebra's management plans to expand its distribution network further in the Visayas and Mindanao Areas and intensify its marketing campaigns in those areas. This is a prudent choice as their retail partners are still underpenetrated in those areas and could have a growth potential once economic conditions in those areas improve. Furthermore, the price range that Ginebra competes in is perfect for those areas as these underdeveloped places likely won't have the financial capability for premium or upscale spirit brands.

To summarize, I believe this is a magnificent performance from Ginebra. Its operating margin continues to expand as the sales volume continues to increase. With the Philippine GDP growth as a tailwind on its top line, I believe that there will be further growth for Ginebra in the foreseeable future without going international, where it faces intense competition. Additionally, Ginebra's strong profitability, brand equity, and cost leadership due to its economy of scale will continue to be a competitive advantage in the Philippines. Additionally, there is the regulation, which from the outset looks to be a headwind for the Philippine spirit market. However, I believe this regulation actually makes it harder for entrants to challenge Ginebra's dominant position.

How Sin Tax Law Puts Ginebra In A Favorable Competitive Position

On January 22, 2020, Philippine then-President Rodrigo Duterte signed the Sin Tax bill into law. The law's objective is to raise money for the implementation of the Universal Health Act and encourage reduced consumption of cigarettes and Alcohol in the Philippines. The effect of the Sin Tax Law is clearly reflected in Ginebra's gross margin, which has declined since 2020.

This decline is attributed to the annual increase in the excise tax on alcoholic drinks, which accounts for a significant portion of Ginebra's cost of sales.

Despite this disturbing trend on their gross margin, I believe that Ginebra has chosen not to fully pass on the excise tax increase to consumers as they have other measures, especially on their operating side, that can offset their declining gross margin without a significant price increase.

Instead, by maintaining a price that wouldn't abuse the price inelasticity of its products, Ginebra was able to increase its sales volume. This allowed it to spread the cost per unit among its fixed operating expenses, such as maintaining the logistics fleet and marketing campaigns. This resulted in an operating margin expansion for the company.

This is also evident in the chart below, which shows that revenue growth has clearly outpaced the increase in overhead costs and selling and marketing costs.

With the annual increase in excise tax per proof liter set to ease at around 6% per year onwards, I believe there could be room for gross margin expansion for Ginebra moving forward. It could gradually increase its prices as consumer purchasing power continues to increase and inflation normalizes without significantly disrupting the buying tendency of its customers.

Despite the continued effort of the Philippine government to reduce alcohol spirit consumption, which from the outset is a headwind for the industry, these regulatory hurdles actually benefit Ginebra due to its economy of scale and entrenched leadership position in the spirit market. The annual increase in excise tax on alcoholic beverages will make it harder for local and international entrants that lack the scale in the Philippines to be price-competitive, which protects Ginebra's low-cost position. Aside from trying to reach a certain sales volume to achieve the economy of scale needed in manufacturing, which can be a challenge due to Ginebra's market presence and its significant resources and profitability to outinvest its competitors, entrants also need to cover for the fixed operating cost associated with consistent marketing to become and remain relevant and distribution. Just like in manufacturing, logistics must reach a certain scale before it becomes economically feasible to maintain. This further increases the fixed cost that entrants need to cover in order to compete effectively against Ginebra.

I believe that the mass-market price segment of the Philippines spirit market will continue to be dominated by incumbents with dominant market share in their respective spirit categories: Ginebra San Miguel in Gin, Emperador and Alfonso in brandy, and Tanduay in rum. The segment I could see entrants targeting is the premium or upscale one, as they could offset the high fixed cost through premium prices. Ginebra is also positioning itself in this emerging segment as there is a clear trend of Filipinos turning to premium spirit drinks due to the continued increase in the quality of living in the Philippines. While there won't be synergy in marketing and distribution due to the channel differences—one is mass-market, and the other is upscale—Ginebra has the resources and profitability to build its premium offering and, more importantly, the patience since time is very important in nurturing brand equity among consumers, especially the upscale ones that seek exclusivity and uniqueness.

Valuation

With an operating profit of around ₱8.6B and an enterprise value of around ₱71.33B, this translates to around 12% earnings yield or around 8.3x EV/EBIT multiple. Ginebra's balance sheet currently shows a significant cash pile-up, which explains the discrepancy with its current P/E ratio of around 11.75x. Ginebra currently has a cash surplus of around ₱14B if you subtract its total debt from its cash & cash equivalents. However, dividends are also increasing year by year due to the significant amount of cash the operations are generating lately versus their reinvestment rate or the generated cash they are reinvesting back into the business.

Since 2019, Ginebra has been reinvesting an average of around 12% of its operating profit back into the business. If the company keeps generating this amount of cash without reinvestment growth opportunities, there will likely be a big payout in the future. Thus, I believe that this cash in the balance sheet will eventually be paid out to the shareholders through increased dividends or share buybacks. Therefore, I will maintain a 'Buy' rating for Ginebra.

Where To Buy Ginebra San Miguel

Whenever my article is about a Philippine stock, I will put a special section here for investors looking to invest in the Philippine stock market. To invest in the Philippines, you have two options: Invest in Ginebra's OTC through your broker with a stock ticker symbol of 'GBSMF' or directly in the Philippine stock exchange or PSE by depositing your money with a trusted Philippine stockbroker such as COL Financial with a stock ticker of 'GSMI.' I will put this link here; COL Financial provided a guide here for foreign investors. Now, if you can stomach the volatility due to low volume and liquidity and will invest for the long term without looking at the stock price, then go for the ADR. Suppose you like looking at your portfolio every day and can't stand an overnight double-digit decline. In that case, you can invest directly by depositing your money directly with a Philippine broker. The liquidity and volume in the Philippine stock market, especially the highly popular stocks such as Ginebra, is high; the bid-and-ask spread is very close. If you worry about trust, you can go for popular and established stockbrokers such as COL Financial, which has been in operation since 1999. They are one of the most trusted brokers in the Philippines. Also, these stockbrokers are heavily regulated by the Philippine SEC and PSE.

Risk

Majority-Owned Company

Like many other public companies in the Philippines, Ginebra is majority-owned by its parent company or a powerful family, San Miguel Food and Beverage (SMPFF), which is owned by the ultimate parent, San Miguel Corporation (SMGBF). Due to the significant control of the company, they could undergo initiatives that could harm the well-being of the minority holder. A good example is companies piling cash on their balance sheet instead of distributing it back to the shareholders through buybacks or dividends even though there is a clear lack of growth opportunity for that idle cash, which can generate a superior above-cost capital return, which can also negatively affect the company's return on equity. In the US equity market, this is not that common anymore since you have corrective forces such as activist investors, private equity, and hedge funds on the lookout for these arbitrage opportunities, ready to buy a stake in the company and force the board to distribute their cash pile-ups through dividends or share buybacks. In the Philippine equity market, these corrective forces aren't available due to the lack of maturity of the equity market. Another good example is companies that don't do anything to unlock shareholder value, which can lead to their stock price declining to a deep discount level, even though the underlying business fundamentals remain strong. The company will instead choose to go private and buy back the shares of its minority shareholders at a deep discount. This is why I only invest in businesses that have a dividend policy and a yield that is competitive with the risk-free rate in the Philippines. The dividend yield will usually support the price from a further downside as long as the company can support that payout.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GigaCloud: No Bottom In Sight - More Uncertainties Ahead
data.description:
GCT's Prospects Remains Mixed, Attributed To The Uncertain Macro/Geopolitical Environment

We previously covered GigaCloud Technology Inc. (GCT) in December 2024, discussing its robust performance metrics and growing profitability, as its new Branding As A Service [BAAS] took off despite the seasonal fluctuation in furniture sales.

Despite the growing GMV, active buyers/sellers, and richer balance sheet, it was unsurprising that the stock continued to be discounted, attributed to the potential US tariff increases along with its operations in China, worsened by the still elevated short interest.

GCT 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, our concerns have materialized as GCT further pulled back by -40.4%, well underperforming the wider market at -11.2% and its well diversified commerce peers to a large degree.

It is apparent that the market has already turned pessimistic surrounding the furniture commerce company, as it fails to hold on to much of its 2024 stock price gains, worsened by the short seller attack in May 2024.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the potentially impacted consumer discretionary spending, thanks to the sticky inflationary pressures and the higher recessionary risks, worsened by the intensified tariff/trade war.

These developments have already triggered the possibility of borrowing costs being higher for longer, with the culmination of pessimistic developments contributing to the market-wide selloff since mid-February 2025, GCT included.

This is despite GCT's consistent outperformance over the past ten consecutive quarters (aside from the slight miss in FQ4 '24 bottom-lines).

This is on top of the robust FQ4 '24 revenues of $295.78M (-2.4% QoQ/+20.8% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 10.4% (-5.6 points QoQ/-7.5 YoY), and adj EPS of $0.75 (-34.7% QoQ/-29.9% YoY) merely suffering from the typical seasonality in large furniture purchases.

Otherwise, given the robust FY2024 numbers of +65.7% YoY, 13.5% (-3.3 points YoY), and +31.8% YoY, respectively, significantly aided by the full year of Noble House acquisition, we believe that GCT has outperformed indeed.

This is especially since GCT continues to expand its Marketplace Gross Merchandise Volume to $1.34B (+68.9% YoY) and 3P seller GigaCloud Marketplace GMV to $693.9M (+62.8% YoY).

This is on top of the growing active 3P sellers to 1,111 (+36.3% YoY) and higher active buyers at 9,306 (+85.7% YoY), well balancing the moderating spend per active buyer at $144.14K (-9% YoY).

Despite GCT's growing inventory levels of $172.48M (+30.4% YoY), readers must note that its inventory turnover ratio has improved to 3.42x in FY2024, compared to a year ago at 2.67x, underscoring its ability to "more efficiently sell the inventory it buys" while "not overspending on inventory purchases and is not incurring high storage and holding costs."

The management has also been diligently diversifying its global footprint, particularly in the EU, where the region reported +150% organic growth YoY in FY2024, building upon the region's "highest revenue growth" in FY2023.

On the other hand, readers may also want to note that the EU only comprises 16.3% of GCT's FY2024 revenues (+5.6 points YoY), with the bulk still attributed to the US at 75.9% (-2.8 points YoY).

With the US still being GCT's growth driver, as observed in the FY2024 revenue growth at +59% YoY/FY2023 at +39% YoY, it is undeniable that the ongoing uncertainties may trigger growth headwinds in FY2025.

This is especially since the new US administration "pushes aides to go bigger on tariffs as the key deadline nears," with the US consumer spending already recording the "first drop in almost two years."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This situation is especially worsened by the GCT's mixed FQ1 '25 revenue guidance of $257.5M at the midpoint (-12.9% QoQ/+5.2% YoY), with it naturally triggering questions surrounding its growth prospects after the robust 5Y historical top/bottom-line (adj EBITDA) expansion at a CAGR of +56.8%/+95%, respectively.

This is despite the management highlighting the minimal tariff impact on large and bulky furniture at supposedly "very, very low-single digit," with the sequential headwinds attributed to the macroeconomic uncertainties and the seasonality of its top-line driver segment, outdoor furniture sales.

Even so, given the tougher YoY comparisons after the accretive Noble House acquisition in November 2023, it appears that we are entering a painful growth normalization in 2025.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have lowered their forward estimates, with GCT expected to chart a decelerating top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +4.2%/+2.2% through FY2026, respectively, compared to the original estimates of +19.6%/+38.7%.

On the other hand, GCT has been able to report rich Free Cash Flow generation of $142.54M in FY2024 (+10.4% YoY), with the same expected over the next two years.

Combined with the inherent lack of debt and richer balance sheet at a cash position of $302.42M (+65% YoY), it is unsurprising that the management has been able to retire -1% of its float at an average price of $22.13 per share since the authorization of its new $46M share repurchase program on September 03, 2024.

With GCT trading way below those numbers, it appears that the management may possibly aggressively apply more of those repurchase authorizations at current levels, naturally returning great value to long-term shareholders.

So, Is GCT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

GCT 2Y Stock Price

Even so, it is undeniable that GCT has had a painful -65.1% correction since the 2024 top, with it effectively losing -$1.2B of its market cap while consistently trading below its 50/100/200 day moving averages.

GCT Valuations

The same ongoing discount has been observed in GCT's FWD P/E GAAP valuations of 5.86x, down from the 1Y mean of 7.91x, the 2024 peaks of 18.76x, and the sector median of 16.37x.

GCT's oversold status has also been observed after comparing its FWD PEG GAAP ratio of 0.15x, against the sector median of 0.72x and its well diversified commerce peers, including Amazon (AMZN) at 0.39x, MercadoLibre (MELI) at 0.55x, and Sea Limited (SE) at 0.93x, with it underscoring the market's uncertainties surrounding the former's prospects.

If anything, despite the steep selloff thus far, its short interest ratio is still inflated at 20.7% as the CBOE Volatility Index remains elevated beyond the prior 125 trading days, with it signaling further volatility ahead.

Combined with the lack of floor from the meltdown and the ongoing macroeconomic/geopolitical headwinds, we believe that it may be more prudent to observe GCT from the sidelines for now, resulting in our reiterated Hold (Neutral) rating.
